skill.name: dbos-python
skill.description: DBOS Python SDK for building reliable, fault-tolerant applications with durable workflows. Use this skill when writing Python code with DBOS, creating workflows and steps, using queues, using DBOSClient from external applications, or building applications that need to be resilient to failures.
skill.summary: Build reliable Python applications with DBOS durable workflows, steps, queues, and fault-tolerant execution.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 483 static findings are false positives. The skill is a documentation guide for the DBOS Python SDK. Detected patterns (external commands, weak crypto, Windows SAM) are markdown code fences, documentation text, and unrelated keywords - not actual security issues. No malicious behavior detected.

content.user_title: Build Reliable Python Apps with DBOS
content.value_statement: DBOS Python provides durable workflow execution that automatically recovers from failures. Write workflows that survive crashes, restarts, and retries without data loss or corruption.
actual_capabilities: Create durable workflows that automatically recover from failures
Define workflow steps with automatic retry logic
Implement queues for concurrency control and rate limiting
Handle workflow communication via events, messages, and streams
Test workflows with built-in test fixtures
limitations: Requires PostgreSQL database for workflow state storage
Workflows must be deterministic - cannot use random or external time
Limited to Python applications, not language-agnostic
Cannot call external APIs directly in workflows without steps

use_cases.title: Build resilient microservices
use_cases.description: Create microservices that automatically retry failed operations and maintain data consistency across distributed systems.

use_cases.title: Implement reliable job processing
use_cases.description: Process background jobs with guaranteed execution, automatic retries, and exactly-once semantics using queues.

use_cases.title: Create long-running business workflows
use_cases.description: Build multi-step business processes that survive infrastructure failures and maintain audit trails.

prompt_templates.title: Add DBOS to existing Python app
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an existing Python FastAPI application. Show me how to add DBOS for durable workflow execution. Include the setup steps, how to convert a function to a workflow, and how to test it.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding durable execution to an existing project

prompt_templates.title: Create a retry-enabled workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow that calls an external API. Include step definitions with retry configuration, error handling, and compensation logic for failures.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a workflow with external dependencies

prompt_templates.title: Implement queue-based processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement a queue with concurrency limits, priority levels, and rate limiting using DBOS queues. Include the producer and consumer code.

prompt_templates.title: Test DBOS workflows
prompt_templates.prompt: Write tests for a DBOS workflow that verifies retry behavior, workflow idempotency, and transaction rollback. Use the DBOS test fixtures.

output_examples.input: How do I create a workflow with automatic retries?
output_examples.output: Define a step with retry configuration using the step decorator. The workflow will automatically retry failed steps according to your policy.
Initialize DBOS in your FastAPI app startup event. Register the DBOS middleware and use the DBOS decorator on your endpoint handlers.
Use SetWorkflowTimeout to prevent runaway workflows. Timed-out workflows are cancelled and can be resumed.

best_practices: Keep workflows deterministic by avoiding random values and external time calls
Use idempotent steps to enable safe retries without duplicate side effects
Configure appropriate timeouts for long-running workflows to prevent resource leaks
Test workflows with various failure scenarios including crashes and network failures
Store critical workflow state in the workflow context, not external variables
anti_patterns: Using random values or current time directly in workflows - breaks determinism
Calling external APIs directly in workflow body instead of in steps - loses retry capability
Not setting timeouts on long-running workflows - risks resource exhaustion
Skipping workflow testing with failure injection - won't catch reliability issues

faq.question: What is DBOS?
faq.answer: DBOS (Database-Oriented Operating System) is a Python framework that provides durable workflow execution. Workflows automatically recover from failures without data loss.

faq.question: How does DBOS handle failures?
faq.answer: DBOS records every workflow step to a database. If a workflow crashes or is interrupted, it automatically resumes from the last completed step when restarted.

faq.question: What databases does DBOS support?
faq.answer: DBOS requires PostgreSQL for storing workflow state and execution history. It does not support other databases in the current version.

faq.question: Can I use DBOS with FastAPI?
faq.answer: Yes, DBOS provides built-in FastAPI integration. You can initialize DBOS in your FastAPI app and use decorators on your route handlers.

faq.question: How do DBOS queues work?
faq.answer: DBOS queues provide reliable message processing with concurrency limits, priority levels, and rate limiting. Messages are persisted to the database for durability.

faq.question: What are the key constraints for DBOS workflows?
faq.answer: Workflows must be deterministic - they cannot use random values, external time, or non-deterministic operations. All external calls must happen in steps with retry logic.
